statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, determine if it is sorted in ascending order. The array can contain duplicate elements.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer n, the number of elements in the array. The second line contains n integers.
statement.outputFormat: Print 'true' if the array is sorted in ascending order, 'false' otherwise.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array.

examples.explanation: The array is sorted in ascending order.
The array is not sorted in ascending order.
The array is sorted in ascending order.
The array is sorted in ascending order.

hints.text: To determine if an array is sorted, we need to compare each element with its next one.
We can iterate through the array and check if each element is less than or equal to the next one.
The key pattern here is to use a simple iteration and keep track of whether we have found any pair of elements that are out of order.

editorial.title: Checking if an Array is Sorted
editorial.intuition: The problem requires determining if the elements of an array are in non-decreasing order. A brute force approach would involve comparing every pair of adjacent elements, which is inherently correct but inefficient for large arrays.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force solution would involve iterating through the array and comparing each element with its next one. If at any point, an element is greater than its successor, the array is not sorted. This approach is correct but has a time complexity of O(n) and does not optimize for space, making it inefficient for very large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach leverages Python's built-in functions to simplify the code while maintaining efficiency.
editorial.optimalSolution: The provided reference solution utilizes Python's built-in 'all' function in combination with a generator expression to check if all adjacent elements in the array are in non-decreasing order. This approach is both time and space efficient, achieving a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(1).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that the 'all' function returns True if and only if all elements of the iterable (in this case, the generator expression) are true. The generator expression (nums[i] <= nums[i + 1] for i in range(len(nums) - 1)) generates a sequence of boolean values indicating whether each pair of adjacent elements is in non-decreasing order. Therefore, if all pairs of adjacent elements are in non-decreasing order, the array is sorted.
editorial.dryRun: For an array [1, 2, 3, 4], the generator expression will yield [True, True, True], and 'all' will return True, correctly indicating the array is sorted.
commonMistakes: Assuming a sorting algorithm is needed to solve the problem, which would increase the time complexity to O(n log n).
Overlooking the possibility of using built-in functions to simplify the code.
Not considering the space complexity, leading to solutions that use extra space unnecessarily.
Incorrectly handling edge cases, such as arrays with duplicate elements or arrays with negative numbers.
Implementing a solution that does not short-circuit as soon as it finds a pair of elements out of order.
